records.ety: [OE. either, aither, AS. ǣgðer, ǣghwæðer (akin to OHG. ēogiwedar, MHG. iegeweder); ā + ge + hwæðer whether. See Each, and Whether, and cf. Or, conj.]

defs: One of two; the one or the other; -- properly used of two things, but sometimes of a larger number, for any one.
quotes: Lepidus flatters both, Of both is flattered; but he neither loves, Nor either cares for him.
Scarce a palm of ground could be gotten by either of the three.
There have been three talkers in Great British, either of whom would illustrate what I say about dogmatists.

defs: Each of two; the one and the other; both; -- formerly, also, each of any number.
quotes: His flowing hair In curls on either cheek played.
On either side . . . was there the tree of life.
The extreme right and left of either army never engaged.

defs: Either precedes two, or more, coördinate words or phrases, and is introductory to an alternative. It is correlative to or.
quotes: Either he is talking, or he is pursuing, or he is in a journey, or peradventure he sleepeth.
Few writers hesitate to use either in what is called a triple alternative; such as, We must either stay where we are, proceed, or recede.
Can the fig tree, my brethren, bear olive berries? either a vine, figs?
notes: ☞ Either was formerly sometimes used without any correlation, and where we should now use or.
